skill.name: routeros-hotspot
skill.description: RouterOS hotspot captive portal for wired/wireless access control. Use when: configuring hotspot on RouterOS, setting up captive portal, writing hotspot profiles or instances, configuring walled garden, setting DHCP option 114 (RFC 8910 captive portal URI), integrating RADIUS with hotspot, or when the user mentions /ip/hotspot, walled-garden, hotspot profile, or captive portal on MikroTik.
skill.summary: Master RouterOS hotspot configuration including captive portal, profiles, external authentication, and RADIUS integration
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 673 potential issues but ALL are false positives. The skill is documentation reference material for RouterOS hotspot configuration. All 'external command' detections are markdown inline code formatting and RouterOS template variable syntax documentation. 'Weak crypto' flags are legitimate RouterOS CHAP/MD5 authentication documentation. 'Hardcoded IPs' use RFC 5737 TEST-NET addresses. No executable code, no credential exfiltration, no malicious patterns. This is a pure documentation skill.

content.value_statement: Network administrators struggle with RouterOS hotspot complexity including captive portal flow, HTTPS login pages, and RADIUS integration. This skill provides complete configuration patterns for MikroTik hotspot setup from profiles to external authentication.
actual_capabilities: Configure RouterOS hotspot profiles with HTTPS login, MAC authentication, and custom HTML directory
Set up hotspot instances bound to interfaces with address pools and session limits
Configure DHCP option 114 (RFC 8910) for native captive portal detection on iOS and Android
Implement walled garden rules for unauthenticated access to specific hosts or domains
Integrate RADIUS authentication with fallback servers and secret management patterns
Build external captive portal flows with template variable substitution
limitations: Hotspot is IPv4-only; IPv6 clients are not supported by the hotspot subsystem
Cannot modify dynamically injected hotspot firewall chains (hs-unauth, hs-auth)
External auth requires separate web server for authentication logic

use_cases.title: Hotel or Cafe WiFi Setup
use_cases.description: Configure a branded captive portal with HTTPS login page where guests accept terms before accessing the internet through your MikroTik router.

use_cases.title: Corporate Hotspot with RADIUS
use_cases.description: Integrate hotspot authentication with existing RADIUS server (Active Directory, FreeRADIUS) so employees use their corporate credentials to access the network.

use_cases.title: Managed WiFi Service
use_cases.description: Deploy external captive portal that validates vouchers or payment before granting internet access, with RADIUS pass-through for session limits and quotas.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Hotspot Profile
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RouterOS hotspot profile named 'guest-profile' that serves HTTPS login at 10.20.0.1 with DNS name login.wifi.example.com, uses MAC authentication as username and password, and enables RADIUS accounting.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up a basic guest WiFi hotspot

prompt_templates.title: DHCP Option 114 for Captive Portal
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure DHCP option 114 on my-dhcp-server to advertise the captive portal URI at https://login.wifi.example.com/api using the force=yes flag.
prompt_templates.scenario: Ensuring iOS and Android devices automatically show the captive portal

prompt_templates.title: Walled Garden for External Auth
prompt_templates.prompt: Add walled garden IP entries to allow unauthenticated access to auth.example.com and *.auth.example.com for my hotspot named hotspot1.
prompt_templates.scenario: Configuring external authentication provider access before login

prompt_templates.title: RADIUS with Failover
prompt_templates.prompt: Add two RADIUS servers at 198.51.100.10 and 198.51.100.11 for hotspot authentication with a 3-second timeout. Create them disabled and enable after the hotspot files are ready.

output_examples.input: Create a hotspot profile with HTTPS login and MAC authentication
output_examples.output: Profile with name 'corp-profile' at 192.168.100.1, DNS name login.corp.internal, SSL certificate corp-wifi.crt_0, login-by set to https and mac with mac-as-username-and-password mode, use-radius enabled, and html-directory-override pointing to hotspot-files

output_examples.input: Configure DHCP option 114 for captive portal detection
output_examples.output: Option code 114 with force=yes, value set to "'https://login.example.com/api'", added to captive-portal-set and assigned to the DHCP server

output_examples.input: Add RADIUS servers for hotspot with comments for management
output_examples.output: Two /radius entries at 198.51.100.10 and 198.51.100.11 with service=hotspot, timeout=3s, disabled=yes until hotspot files are ready, with comment tags for idempotent management

best_practices: Use HTTPS login-by and install SSL certificates to prevent credential exposure on the login page
Always use the -esc variants of template variables when embedding values into URLs or query strings
Create RADIUS entries disabled and enable them only after hotspot HTML files and certificates are ready
anti_patterns: Adding DROP firewall rules for port 443 on the hotspot interface breaks the HTTPS login page silently
Using keepalive-timeout=0 instead of none has no effect; keepalive stays enabled
Adding /ip/hotspot/user entries when use-radius=yes has no effect since local users are bypassed

faq.question: Why does my HTTPS login page not work even though I added firewall rules to allow it?
faq.answer: Hotspot traffic intercept runs before firewall input/forward chains. Firewall DROP rules for port 443 from the hotspot interface will silently break the HTTPS login page because hotspot handles it first.

faq.question: Why do iOS and Android devices not show the captive portal even though I configured option 114?
faq.answer: Clients silently skip DHCP option 114 if it is not in their Parameter Request List. You must add force=yes to the option configuration to ensure all clients receive it.

faq.question: Can I use IPv6 with RouterOS hotspot?
faq.answer: No. RouterOS hotspot relies on NAT and is IPv4-only. IPv6 clients are not supported by the hotspot subsystem and will not be properly handled.

faq.question: Why do my /ip/hotspot/user entries not work even though I added them?
faq.answer: When use-radius=yes is set in the hotspot profile, local /ip/hotspot/user entries are completely bypassed. All authentication goes through RADIUS. Remove use-radius or add users to your RADIUS server instead.

faq.question: How do I allow HTTPS sites before authentication?
faq.answer: Use /ip/hotspot/walled-garden/ip for HTTPS destinations. The standard /ip/hotspot/walled-garden only works with HTTP URL pattern matching and cannot inspect HTTPS traffic.

faq.question: Why is the login page showing the wrong certificate?
faq.answer: RouterOS appends _0 to imported certificate names. Use the full name like login.example.com.crt_0 in both the hotspot profile ssl-certificate setting and the /ip/service/www-ssl certificate setting.
